summary: High-risk regulatory changes impacting food seasoning/snack compliance include: EU introduces new microbiology limits for Salmonella (specific countries) and general foodborne bacteria/toxins. GCC/GSO mandates updates to general, Halal, and shelf-life labeling, alongside new microbiological limits. Singapore implements stricter rules for food additives (mandatory compliance, SFA assessment, prohibition of unpermitted) and contaminants (mandatory compliance, specific limits, prohibition of exceeding limits, and specific veterinary drug residues). New mandatory declaration of ingredients/additives and general labeling compliance are also in effect for Singapore. Taiwan now requires mandatory allergen declarations for specific items (e.g., shellfish, peanuts, milk, gluten, soy, fish) and sulphite (≥10 mg/kg). Thailand has new compliance requirements for general food/nutrition labeling and prohibits certain foods. The United States has new requirements for prohibited additives, mandatory declaration of major food allergens (including sesame), specific contaminant limits (3-MCPD, Aflatoxins), and detailed labeling rules for 'gluten-free' claims, allergen cross-contact, cochineal/carmine, specific allergen types, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents (≥10 ppm).

changes.country: European Union (Sweden, Finland, Norway, and other EU countries seeking equivalent status)
changes.notes: Special guarantees for Salmonella control are provided for Sweden, Finland, and Norway concerning trade of certain live animals and products. Other EU countries can obtain equivalent status by implementing approved control programs as per referenced regulations.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii), their toxins and metabolites (e.g., staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.notes: Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005 lays down food safety criteria and process hygiene criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins, and metabolites in specific foods.

changes.parameter: General labelling requirements for pre-packaged foodstuffs
changes.notes: This is a technical regulation, mandatory for pre-packaged food products.
This is a technical regulation, mandatory for products claiming to be Halal.

changes.parameter: Mandatory shelf life periods for food products
changes.notes: This is a technical regulation, mandatory for food products.
Verification of specific labelling requirements is required by consulting the full GSO 9:2022 regulation.
Verification of specific general requirements for Halal food is required by consulting the full GSO 2055-1:2015 regulation.
Verification of specific mandatory expiration date requirements is required by consulting the full GSO 150-1:2013 regulation.
Verification of specific microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients is required by consulting the full GSO 1016:2015 regulation.
All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with the regulatory limits on food additives.

changes.oldLimit: Must be assessed for safety by SFA
changes.notes: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.
Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.
Do not import or sell food products containing an additive that is not permitted.
All food businesses must comply with the regulatory limits on incidental constituents (contaminants) in food.
If laboratory test results indicate contaminants, they must be within the specified limits.

changes.oldLimit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
changes.notes: Contaminants (also known as incidental constituents) in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
Do not import or sell food products containing contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed regulatory limits.
Regulation 33 of the Food Regulations prohibits the import or sale of meat and any food derived from meat which contains residues of diethylstibestrol, hexoestrol, or dienoestrol.
For prepacked foods, all ingredients and additives used must be declared in the list of ingredients on food labels.
Food or food products must comply with general labelling requirements.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.notes: These allergens must be declared if present as an ingredient or food additive in prepackaged foods.

changes.newLimit: Mandatory declaration if 10 or more mg/kg
changes.notes: Sulphite must be declared if the residue sulphur dioxide is 10 or more milligrams per kilogram of food.
Food products must comply with general food labeling and nutrition labeling requirements, which implicitly covers allergen declarations and salt/sodium information.
Foods that are prohibited for production, import, or sale must not be handled.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from use in food. Verification required against FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189).
changes.notes: Also includes delisted color additives (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30).

changes.newLimit: milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, sesame
changes.notes: Identified by the Food Allergen Labeling and Consumer Protection Act of 2004 (FALCPA) and the FASTER Act.
Guidance Level.
Action Level.

changes.newLimit: Must meet FDA's defined standard for gluten content. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods also apply.
changes.notes: Helps consumers with celiac disease.

changes.newLimit: Facilities must implement allergen preventive controls as per CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117), including written procedures to control cross-contact and ensure accurate labeling.
changes.notes: Products found to inadvertently contain allergens due to cross-contact are considered misbranded or adulterated.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared on the label.
changes.notes: Identified as allergenic substances derived from insects.

changes.parameter: Declaration of Major Food Allergens (Contains Statement)
changes.newLimit: Must appear immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a “contains” statement.
changes.notes: Example: “Contains wheat, milk, and soy.”

changes.parameter: Declaration of Major Food Allergens (Ingredient List)
changes.newLimit: Must appear in parentheses following the name of the ingredient.
changes.notes: Example: “lecithin (soy),” “flour (wheat),” and “whey (milk)”.

changes.newLimit: Type of tree nut, species of fish, and species of Crustacean shellfish must be declared.
changes.notes: Example: almonds, pecans, walnuts for tree nuts; bass, flounder, cod for fish; crab, lobster, shrimp for Crustacean shellfish.
Due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
Must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration is ≥10 parts per million total sulfites, due to adverse reactions in sensitive individuals (e.g., asthma).

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA) Online Database
changes.newLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.notes: The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions for permitted food additives in all foods. Users are encouraged to consult the database and its Preamble for specific provisions.

changes.newLimit: Verification required via Codex standards and CCCF decisions
changes.notes: Codex establishes Maximum Levels (MLs) and Guideline Levels for contaminants, which are legally permitted maximum concentrations. The General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995) is the primary reference.

changes.newLimit: Adherence to Code of Practice for Prevention and Reduction
changes.notes: A specific Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) exists for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices.
